result.markdown: --- title: "Reference Summary" vignette: > %\VignetteIndexEntry{Reference Summary} %\VignetteEngine{knitr::rmarkdown} %\VignetteEncoding{UTF-8} --- ## What it checks The `ref_summary` module does not run a check of its own. Instead it **collects the results of the other reference-section modules** — [accuracy](mod-ref-accuracy.qmd), [PubPeer](mod-ref-pubpeer.qmd), [replication](mod-ref-replication.qmd), and [retraction](mod-ref-retraction.qmd) — into a single table with one row per reference, so you can see everything known about each cited work in one place. Because it summarises the output of those modules, `ref_summary` must run **after** them, in the same report. On its own it has nothing to summarise. ::: callout-note The modules it summarises make **live network calls**, so an internet connection is needed. ::: ## Running the module The summary needs the other reference modules to have run first. The natural way to arrange that is to run them together with `report_module_run()`, which keeps each module's output and passes the chain along (see [Creating a Metacheck Report](creating-a-report.qmd)). List `ref_summary` last: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} paper <- demopaper() module_output <- report_module_run( paper, modules = c("ref_accuracy", "ref_pubpeer", "ref_replication", "ref_retraction", "ref_summary") ) mo <- module_output[["ref_summary"]] mo$traffic_light ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` #> [1] "info" ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} mo$summary_text ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` #> [1] "Summary information provided for 5 references" ``` ::: ::: The combined `table` has one row per reference and one column per upstream check: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} mo$table[, c("bib_id", "accuracy_mismatch", "pubpeer", "replication_type", "retractionwatch")] |> knitr::kable() ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} | bib_id|accuracy_mismatch |pubpeer |replication_type |retractionwatch | |------:|:-----------------|:--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|:----------------|:---------------| | 0|no match |NA |NA |NA | | 1|NA |NA |replication |NA | | 2|title |Link |NA |Retraction | | 3|doi, author |NA |NA |NA | | 4|no match |NA |NA |NA | ::: ::: A reference is interesting when any of these columns is filled in: a reported accuracy mismatch, a PubPeer comment, a replication outcome, or a Retraction Watch entry. Follow up those references using the dedicated chapter for whichever column flagged it. ## Interpreting the result `ref_summary` inherits everything from the modules it summarises, so the same cautions apply: a filled-in cell is a prompt to look, not a verdict. Its value is convenience — instead of reading four separate tables, you scan one. The summary itself is informational (`info`); the underlying modules carry the traffic lights. ## Options `ref_summary` takes only the `paper` argument, but it only produces useful output when the other `ref_*` modules have run before it in the same report.
